edge: A permutation π is considered a causal ordering of a structural causal model (SCM) M if, for every variable xi that directly causes xj, the index of xi in the permutation is less than the index of xj.

Structural causal models (SCMs) enable causal inference by allowing answers to what-if questions related to forcing fixed values on variables (interventional queries) and exploring the effects of changing values of dimensions on specific observations (counterfactual queries).

Two matrices S and R are considered structurally equivalent if they have zeroes in the same positions. Additionally, one matrix is deemed structurally sparser than another if every zero element in the second matrix corresponds to a zero element in the first matrix.

Recursive SCM is a representation of an SCM where the equations are unrolled by having multiple functions zl = fl(zl−1), and in each one the equations for which the non-recursive equation of its parents is already known are unrolled.

Unrolled SCM is a representation of an SCM where the equations are unrolled by having multiple functions zl = fl(zl−1), and the equations for which the non-recursive equation of its parents is already known are unrolled.

Collapsed SCM is a representation of an SCM where all the different layers are expanded and written in a (probably complex) formula that encompasses all changes in a single step. The composition of these functions has a Jacobian matrix structurally equivalent to I + diam(A).

Reverse SCM is a representation of an SCM where each ˜fi(xpai, ui) is assumed to be bijective with respect to ui, allowing the computation of its inverse to obtain ui as a function of the observed values.
The inverse is computed to obtain ui as a function of the observed values, ui = ˜f(xpai, xi).
The Jacobian matrix of the inverse will be structurally equivalent to I + A ≡ I − A.
Jacobian matrices are used to talk about causal dependencies between variables.

Causal NF consistently outperforms CAREFL in terms of performance and computational efficiency. Causal NF achieves similar performance to CAREFL in terms of observational fitting, while surpassing it on interventional and counterfactual estimation tasks. Additionally, Causal NF outperforms CAREFL in computational speed.
Causal NF consistently outperforms VACA in terms of performance and computational efficiency. The performance of VACA is notably inferior, and its computation time is significantly longer.

Causal NF has a single layer, reducing computational complexity.
